turns.content:
I'm trying to port our microcontroller code and I thought adding #![no_std] would just strip away the standard library like in C, so I can use all my existing std crates without any changes. That should make it simpler right?

So I can't just drop std and keep using my normal Rust code? It seems like I'd have to rewrite everything because core doesn't have collections or error handling built-in?

Exactly, that's why bare-metal development is harder. Core provides the basic building blocks like Result, Option, and panic! but lacks higher-level abstractions you're used to in std. You need to implement your own error types using core::panic! instead of std::error::Error, and manage memory without automatic garbage collection since there's no heap allocator by default. It's like trying to cook a full meal with only basic kitchen tools—you can still make it work, but you have to build every component yourself.
I'm getting confused about the panic handler too. If I just use core::panic! everywhere and don't set up my own unwinding logic, won't the program just crash immediately without any cleanup?

You're right to be concerned; in a no_std environment, panics are much more dangerous because they terminate the program abruptly without calling destructors. You must implement a custom panic handler that catches these exceptions and allows your application to continue running or shut down gracefully. Think of it like building a fire extinguisher system: if you don't have one, every small spark becomes a full-blown fire that burns everything down. Your panic handler acts as the safety mechanism that prevents total collapse when things go wrong.

I'm trying to understand the fundamental difference between writing Rust with standard library access versus a bare-metal setup. Specifically, I want to know how removing std affects what functions are available.

When you enable #![no_std] and #![no_main], the compiler strips away all features from the standard library like panic hooks or error handling. You must then implement your own panic handler using core::panic::PanicInfo instead of relying on std's default behavior.

That sounds restrictive, but I see it in my project now. I'm building a small tool that doesn't need the standard library. Can you explain exactly what I lose and how to handle things like memory allocation without std::alloc?

Without std, you lose everything from Vec, HashMap, and panic hooks until you define them yourself. For example, instead of calling alloc::vec or std::collections::VecDeque, you must use core::mem::take or implement your own allocator using the heap API provided by the microcontroller.
Okay, so I need to manually manage memory. But what about error handling? Can't I just wrap my code in try blocks?

Try blocks require std::result::Result types which aren't available by default. You must implement the Result trait yourself using core::ops::ControlFlow or similar patterns, and handle panics manually via your custom panic handler.
I'm struggling with that part. It feels like I have to write everything from scratch instead of just importing crates. Is there a way to make this easier?

Think of it like building an engine vs driving a car. In no_std, you're writing the engine yourself using core primitives, whereas with std you get pre-built components. You can still use crates like esp-hal or cortex-m-rt which provide essential abstractions on top of bare-metal.
So I should just rely on those crates instead of trying to write the panic handler myself?

Exactly, but understanding how #![no_std] works under the hood helps you debug why your code fails. For instance, if you forget to export a type or trait from core::intrinsics, compilation will fail immediately because there's no std to catch it.

I keep reading about writing #![no_std] tools for embedded systems but I still don't get why we can't just use std and panic. My current script has a main function that panics on division by zero, but in production this crashes the whole device instead of printing an error message. I've tried adding dependencies like serde_json but it won't compile because there's no allocator.

The core issue is that std requires a heap and panic handlers, which don't exist on bare metal without extra setup. In #![no_std] mode you must replace panic with a custom recovery routine using the #[panic_handler] attribute or impl core::panics::PanicHandler trait to catch unwinding. You also need to implement core::alloc::Global for memory management since there's no allocator built in like in std. Without these, any unhandled panic will abort execution immediately.
So I should manually define the panic handler and implement Global? But what if I need something from std like error formatting or logging? Can't I just re-export those traits?

You can't directly use std types because they depend on core::panic::PanicInfo and other std internals that aren't available. Instead, you must implement your own error representation using core::fmt::Argument or core::error::Error traits without relying on std's panic propagation. For logging, you'd write a simple buffer-based system using core::ptr::write to a static array instead of writing to stdout which won't work in no_std environments.
That makes sense but I'm still confused about the difference between core and std. If I don't use #![no_std], why would I need to import anything from core? Isn't everything already included?

When you omit #![no_std], Rust automatically includes std, which is built on top of core. But for embedded systems you want minimal dependencies so you must explicitly use core directly without the std layer. The key difference is that core provides low-level primitives like ptr and panic while std adds higher-level abstractions like collections and standard library functions. In #![no_std] mode, you only have access to core unless you add specific crates manually.
